sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Upper respiratory infection and positive COVID test.
History of Present Illness
Logan is a 57-year-old male with a past medical history of coronary artery bypass grafting, hyperlipidemia, and OCD. He presents with an upper respiratory infection, reporting that about a week ago he started to get a cold, and in the last two or three days it seemed to move into his chest. He reports some post-nasal drip at the beginning of the week, but it has now resolved. He thought he had a slight fever, but no. He denies any fever now. He reports pain in his left hand. He has been able to exercise before he got sick. He traveled over the last week or so, and the diet was not great. He has received both stages of the COVID vaccine. He reports no chest pain, shortness of breath, or other symptoms. He has been taking crestor 20 milligrams a day and reports no muscle aches. He has been able to exercise before he got sick. He has no other sick household members.
Findings
Oxygenation is 96%. Cervical lymphadenopathy is present. Bilateral ronchi are present in the lungs. Wheezing is present. The patient is positive for COVID.
Assessment
The patient has a cold and a positive COVID test.
Plan

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value: The doctor orders a lipid panel in six months. The doctor advises the patient to quarantine for 10 days. The patient should continue taking crestor 20 milligrams a day. Arrange follow-up in four weeks if symptoms persist.
